A strong line of thunderstorms developed along the leading edge of the boundary causing sporadic strong winds that downed a few trees and power lines across portions of northeast Texas and northwest Louisiana.  Cloud to ground lightning was very intense as well and lightning resulted in scattered power outages in southwest Arkansas and northeast Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree down along Hwy. 110 just south of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of severe thunderstorms developed over the northern Big Country during the evening of the first producing mainly large hail. Rainfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches also produced some flooding.

A nearly-stationary upper-level low positioned over western Texas created an environment conducive to three distinct severe weather episodes over eastern Texas. Monday morning's (3/12) fast-moving squall line was initiated from a shortwave rounding the base of the upper low. Tuesday's (3/13) scattered convection was a second shortwave riding up from the west southwest around the still quasi-stationary upper low. Wednesday's (3/14) events stemmed from the upper-low moving eastward over the region into Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on one home with numerous downed tree limbs. National Weather Service in League City estimated wind gusts between 50 and 60 mph during the height of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed along a cold front over much of South Central Texas and in advance of an upper level low moving eastward across New Mexico.

An upper level low moving eastward across the Midwest U.S. spawned several supercells as well as short lines of thunderstorms along a north-south aligned cold front as it crossed the Hill Country and approached the IH-35 corridor.  The storms produced heavy rainfall, large hail, and two tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 3 inches of rain fell at Payton in the late morning, closing SH32 and FM165 due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system remained over west and central Texas for several days.  Numerous shortwaves traveled from this system and over north Texas.  This energy combined with ample moisture to create several rain events over much of the region.  A large amount of rain in a short time triggered flash flooding in many counties.  A few hail and wind reports were received as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported at Highway 84 and FM 1366, and FM 1365 was washed out.  A car was submerged at Highway 75 North near FM 80.  The bridge at W.L. Moody Reunion Grounds was also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system remained over west and central Texas for several days.  Numerous shortwaves traveled from this system and over north Texas.  This energy combined with ample moisture to create several rain events over much of the region.  A large amount of rain in a short time triggered flash flooding in many counties.  A few hail and wind reports were received as well.

The atmosphere became moderately unstable south of a northward moving warm front which moved across northeast Texas and northwest Louisiana during the afternoon and evening hours of April 13th. Thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were downed 3 miles east of the Wood County line along Hwy 154.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the early morning hours of March 31, both supercells and short bow echoes began to form along the north-south aligned cold front after it crossed the IH-35 corridor.  Moving into the eastern portion of South Central Texas, these storms produced heavy rainfall, large hail, and two tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms stretching from northern Fayette County southward through Lavaca and DeWitt Counties produced widespread 1 to 2 inch rain totals as the moved eastward.  Highest totals in DeWitt County were near 4 inches just south of Hochheim. Flash flooding closed SH111 through the early morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled across north Texas...providing focus for afternoon thunderstorms.  There were several reports of hail and flash flooding.
An upper level low pressure system over the west combined with ample moisture over north Texas.  Storms trained over the same areas, creating flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were closed due to flooding, including FM 1915 at Big Elm Creek, FM 443 at Turkey Creek, and FM 407.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving upper level low pressure system resided in the Gulf coast for several days.  A trough extended from this low pressure system into north Texas.  Storms developed during the afternoon hours as the atmosphere destabilized.  Several hail reports were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported near the Hood/Johnson county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact upper level low pressure system to the northwest created ripe conditions for thunderstorm development.  A thunderstorm complex moved into north Texas on the morning of July 10, causing flash flooding across a few counties north of the metroplex.  Storms later redeveloped along boundaries left over from that morning's thunderstorms.  Flash flooding occurred again on the 11th as rain fell on saturated soils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large limbs and fences were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced spotty heavy rainfall on June 26 which turned into flash flooding over parts of the Western Hill Country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced 2 to 3 inch totals from Leakey in Real County eastward to Medina and Vanderpool  in Bandera County.  Highest totals in Real County were 4 inches just east of Leakey.  High water forced the closure of FM337, FM2748, and US83.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm outflow boundary from storms in the Dallas area moved southward into South Central Texas as a second outflow boundary from thunderstorms in the Abilene area moved southeastward.  The two boundaries collided near Marble Falls shortly after midnight on June 26 and produced sustained periods of very heavy rainfall across several counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved into Lee County shortly after midnight and produced a general 1-2 inch rain over the county.  The heaviest amounts were in the Giddings area where 4 inches fell.  Flash flooding was widespread across the central part of the county with many rural roads closed.  Among those closed due to flash flooding were CR226, CR230, and CR231.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dryline set up in west Texas and southeastern New Mexico.  Thunderstorms formed along this dryline and became severe as the afternoon wore on.  Main threat was large hail, the largest reported being nickel size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ward County Sheriff's Office reported penny to nickel size hail falling at the intersection of Farm to Market Roads 1219 and 1776.  This hail fell in a swath from this intersection southeast to Grandfalls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough along the Rocky Mountains and a stationary front across West Central Texas combined to produce widespread showers and thunderstorms across the region.  With the amount of shear in the environment, several of the storms became supercells and produced mainly hail and an isolated tornado near Sweetwater.  For the most part, they remained elevated above the boundary layer. This was the first widespread outbreak of severe thunderstorms for the early spring storm season.
An upper low remained over the area triggering several rounds of showers and thunderstorms.  A very moist environment encouraged flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: FM 209 south of town was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms over the northwest Hill Country on the evening of June 3 and moved southeastward across the north and central sections of South Central Texas through the late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds blew down trees and knocked over power lines between Tarpley and Bandera.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms over Lee County produced flash flooding on June 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced a general 1 to 2 inch rain over southern Lee County.  Highest totals were 4 inches just east of Giddings. FM141 and  FM180 were closed due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the remnants of Tropical Storm Erin continued northward across West Central Texas, additional bands of heavy rainfall formed early on the morning of August 17 and produced additional flooding across portions of the western Texas Hill Country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bands of showers and thunderstorms associated with the remains of Erin formed over the Hill Country in the early morning hours.  One band developed southward into southeastern Blanco County before sunrise.  It produced 3 to 4 inch rain totals that restarted flash flooding over the already saturated soils very quickly in the southeast part of the county.  FM165, FM32, were FM473 reported to be closed due to high water into the mid morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low remained over the area triggering several rounds of showers and thunderstorms.  A very moist environment encouraged flash flooding on top of already saturated grounds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CR 396 was impassable due to high water.  Numerous other county roads were damaged by flood waters and closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system across north Texas allowed for slow moving thunderstorms to develop across portions of northeast Texas during the morning and afternoon hours of June 16th.  These thunderstorms produced copious amounts of rain with widespread flooding noted across some counties of northeast Texas.  Rainfall totals of some three to five inches were reported from these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: TX 135 near FM 2207 was closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low remained over the area triggering several rounds of showers and thunderstorms.  A very moist environment encouraged flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Houses near the Brazos River were flooded as officials were forced to open flood gates at Possum Kingdom Lake.  Some houses were flooded as much as eight feet and were not habitable for a week.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low pressure system situated to the west brought large scale lift to north Texas. Southerly winds brought deep rich moisture from the Gulf of Mexico. These and other factors caused numerous thunderstorms to develop. Widespread flooding and wind damage followed, as well as a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water over numerous roads in western Parker County.  Numerous high water rescues were required.  About 100 residents were evacuated due to flooding.  Numerous homes were damaged by the high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms producing large hail...damaging winds...and tornadoes moved north and northeast across the western and central Texas panhandle during the evening hours. The only injuries occurred in Cactus from a tornado...however no fatalities were reported.
Thunderstorms associated with a persistent upper level low produced heavy rain and flash flooding on the afternoon and evening of July 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 2 and 3 inches of rain fell over Bandera, Kerr, Edwards and Real Counties, with up to 4 inches north of Leakey.  SH41 and FM336 were closed by flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed along a cold front over much of South Central Texas and in advance of an upper level low moving eastward across New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The San Marcos River below Luling crested at 31.75 ft at 0715 CST on March 12, where flood stage is 20 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours produced numerous tornadoes...large hail...prolonged flooding and flash flooding...and damaging winds across the central and northeastern Texas Panhandle. No injuries were reported.

Severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours produced numerous tornadoes...large hail...prolonged flooding and flash flooding...and damaging winds across the central and northeastern Texas Panhandle. No injuries were reported.
Numerous thunderstorms passed across southeastern Texas on the evening of May 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Metro traffic reported half dollar size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of thunderstorms moved in from the southwest and affected Houston metro in the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail reported at the intersection of Jones Road and FM 1960.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms and a tropical atmosphere produced very heavy rainfall and flooding across portions of the southern Coastal Bend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of rain fell across eastern Jim Wells county.  Westbound Hwy 44 was closed from Bentonville to Hwy 359, with water flowing over the road.  San Felipe Creek was also reported flooded in the Rancho Alegre area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours across northeast Texas and northwest Louisiana and quickly became severe producing large hail and damaging thunderstorm gusts. These storms were slow movers and given a very moist atmosphere present at the time across the region...flooding rainfall resulted from the storms across northeast Texas.  A few of these storms developed into southwest Arkansas...producing wind damage as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads underwater from excessive heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms associated with a persistent upper level low produced heavy rain and flash flooding on the afternoon of July 7.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms in southern Williamson County produced between 2 and 3 inches of rain, closing CR214, and CR100, along with several roads in Georgetown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms associated with a persistent upper level low again produced heavy rain and flash flooding in the afternoon of July 20.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gen 3 to 4 inch rainfall was reported in Wilson County, with up to 6 inches north of Stockdale.  US87, SH123, and FM1681 were closed, along with most roads in Stockdale area.  Several homes and businesses were also flooded, with more than a dozen people rescued by emergency services after being trapped in high water in homes and vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms associated with a persistent upper level low again produced heavy rain and flash flooding in the afternoon of July 20.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: General 2 to 3 inch rains fell over Blanco, Gillespie, Kerr and Kendall Counties, with up to 4 inches near south of Stonewall.  FM1376 and Park Road 1 were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms associated with a persistent upper level low produced heavy rain and flash flooding on the afternoon of July 2 in the Hill Country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced between 3 and 4 inches of rain in the Kendalia area, closing FM473 and FM3351 due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A convective system which moved through Oklahoma arrived in north Texas around daybreak on the 30th.  Numerous reports of large hail and wind damage were recieved as well as flash flooding.  Storms formed again on the 31st as a strong low level jet helped initiate storms along boundaries left from storms the day before.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major street flooding was reported in Wolfe City, and a high water rescue was required in Cash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms in the northwest Texas Panhandle during the evening hours produced hail. No damage or injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Time of the hail was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across the southern Big Country during the afternoon and slowly propagated to the southwest. These thunderstorms produced brief tornadoes, large hail and very heavy rain as they moved through the Concho Valley and into the Northern Edwards Plateau. The thunderstorms continued through much of the night causing heavy rains across Sutton, Concho, Menard and McCulloch counties. The heavy rains and flooding continued into the afternoon on the 7th across the Northern Edwards Plateau and the Northwest Hill Country.

An outbreak of severe weather impacted much of the central U.S. from Minnesota to Texas during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 21st. The west Texas South Plains and the extreme southern Texas Panhandle were affected by a series of significant tornadoes. At least five tornadoes were spawned by a single cyclic supercell thunderstorm that resulted in more than $3.5 million in damages.|The most severely impacted communities included Olton (Lamb County) and Tulia (Swisher County). Both of these west Texas towns were impacted by EF-2 tornadoes that destroyed property and injured four people, and more than 14,000 customers were without electrical power throughout the night.|The tornado outbreak was caused by a potent storm system that progressed east over the Four Corners region of the western U.S. on the 21st. A potent upper level jet stream spread over the Southern Plains of the U.S., and helped to create a favorable environment for supercell thunderstorms and tornadoes along a dryline that was stretched across west Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Meteorologists from the National Severe Storms Laboratory reported three inch diameter hail along Interstate 27 north-northwest of Tulia. Very large hail in the Tulia area resulted in damage to vehicles traveling along Interstate 27. In addition, minor damage to young cotton crops was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A split in the upper levels of the atmosphere steered several southern stream impulses into north Texas, producing flash flooding, strong winds, and a few tornadoes.  The most impressive of these events was a derecho which moved through North Texas on May 2.  It produced winds in excess of 85 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A number of barns and sheds had roofs ripped off in the Edna Hill area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours produced numerous tornadoes...large hail...prolonged flooding and flash flooding...and damaging winds across the central and northeastern Texas Panhandle. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado developed over open country about three miles southwest of Lipscomb at 2155 CST. The tornado traveled northeast for about four miles damaging a farm and trees. The tornado dissipated at 2205 CST just northwest of Lipscomb. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours across northeast Texas and northwest Louisiana and quickly became severe producing large hail and damaging thunderstorm gusts. These storms were slow movers and given a very moist atmosphere present at the time across the region...flooding rainfall resulted from the storms across northeast Texas.  A few of these storms developed into southwest Arkansas...producing wind damage as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low water crossing over the Pecos River, nine miles east of the Terrell Gas Plant, was flooded and impassable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60 mph wind gust was reported at the McDonald Observatory. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A round of severe thunderstorms, including a few supercells, impacted portions of the South Plains during the evening hours of the 10th. These storms had a devastating impact on portions of the region's cotton production, with more than 30,000 acres of cotton destroyed by a swath of wind-blown hail over parts of Castro, Hale, and Lamb Counties. Total economic losses totaled more than $30 million. One man was injured when his tractor trailer semi-truck was blown off of Texas Highway 194 near Hart (Castro County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported in New Deal by storm chasers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along an outflow boundary that originated along the SE TX/SW LA gulf coast and moved north during the afternoon/evening hours of 04/02/07.  Intense thunderstorms across SE TX developed a cold pool which helped the boundary continue moving northward. The cold pool aided in additional thunderstorm development as the boundary moved northward with some of the thunderstorms producing strong and damaging wind gusts along with large hail.

Severe thunderstorms developed across the southern Big Country during the afternoon and slowly propagated to the southwest. These thunderstorms produced brief tornadoes, large hail and very heavy rain as they moved through the Concho Valley and into the Northern Edwards Plateau. The thunderstorms continued through much of the night causing heavy rains across Sutton, Concho, Menard and McCulloch counties. The heavy rains and flooding continued into the afternoon on the 7th across the Northern Edwards Plateau and the Northwest Hill Country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail lasted for several minutes. Damage to roofs and cars was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist unstable airmass combined with a weak shortwave caused thunderstorms to develop in the evening hours. There were reports of severe weather scattered across much of West Central Texas, including one tornado across Crockett County.

Severe thunderstorms during the late morning and early afternoon hours produced hail from the northwest Texas panhandle into the south-central Texas panhandle. Severe thunderstorms across the northwest into the southeastern Texas panhandle during the late afternoon through the early morning hours produced large hail...damaging winds...and a tornado. No injuries were reported.
Severe thunderstorms across the southeast Texas panhandle during the early evening hours produced hail and damaging winds. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marble to penny size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low pressure system situated to the west brought large scale lift to north Texas. Southerly winds brought deep rich moisture from the Gulf of Mexico. These and other factors caused numerous thunderstorms to develop. Widespread flooding and wind damage followed, as well as a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water rescues were required near Dry Creek and also Stroud Creek.  A pick-up was swept down Fall Creek.  One of the occupants of the truck swam to safety while the other two clung to a tree and waited for help.  Roads were also flooded in the county.  A woman who's car was being swept away escaped by pulling her convertible top down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 13th, thunderstorms developed near a boundary which had stalled in the area.  On the 14th, a cold front moved into the region causing more flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: FM 489 was barricaded due to high water near Butler, as was FM 80 south of Teague.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough well ahead of a central Texas cold front slowed as it approached the coastal counties. This boundary became the focus for severe thunderstorms along the coast and neighboring inland counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel-size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, mainly eastern county, thunderstorms developed along a near north-south boundary and exhibited very little eastern movement.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree down on a garage in the Montrose area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed ahead of a cold front moving into southeast Texas. The environment was conducive to the formation of large hail and damaging straight line winds. There was a minor threat for isolated tornadoes which prompted a Tornado Watch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on FM 2346.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of strong to severe thunderstorms developed over central and south central Texas as an upper level disturbance approached from the west on the morning of Wednesday, the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sighting located near CR 459 and FM 227 in the community of Demi-John.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across the south central Texas Panhandle during the evening hours. These storms produced hail. No damage or injuries were reported.

A very moist airmass interacted with a mid and upper level weakness to produce showers and thunderstorms and flash flooding across South Texas on the 4th of July.  The heaviest rainfall fell along and northeast of Interstate 37 where a widespread 3 to 6 inches fell.  Isolated amounts of between 6 and 12 inches of rain fell on the 4th across much of Refugio, Calhoun and Aransas counties causing significant, widespread flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 3 and 6 inches of heavy rain fell across portions of central and southern Bee county, resulting in widespread flash flooding of small creeks and low lying roads.  Many secondary roads and highways as well as Farm to Market Roads throguhout the county were flooded.  Water made it into some homes along West Bowie Street in Beeville.  Hardest hit areas in Beeville were streets along the flooded Poesta Creek.  Roughly 20 people were rescued from stranded vehicles and from homes in Beeville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A split in the upper levels of the atmosphere steered several southern stream impulses into north Texas, producing flash flooding, strong winds, and a few tornadoes.  The most impressive of these events was a derecho which moved through North Texas on May 2.  It produced winds in excess of 85 MPH.

Severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon and early evening hours produced hail and high winds across the eastern Texas Panhandle. No injuries were reported...however only some damage to tree branches and gardens had occurred due to the hail.

Severe thunderstorms during the evening hours produced numerous tornadoes and large hail across the southern...central and eastern Texas panhandle. Heavy rains caused flash flooding in the eastern Texas panhandle during the late night and early morning hours. One man was killed by a tornado in the northeastern Texas panhandle and another person was injured.
Heavy tropical rainfall across eastern Jim Wells county and western Bee county resulted in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms trained across eastern Jim Wells county, dumping 3 to 6 inches of rain over saturated ground.  Highway 44 was closed northeast of Alice and west of Agua Dulce, due to both east and west bound lanes flooded with several feet of water in spots.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper level low across West Texas and Eastern New Mexico moved slowly east into West-Central Texas on the 13th.  A short-wave trough in the base of the low tracked north-eastward across the Rio Grande Plains into the Coastal Plains of South Texas during the evening hours.  Large scale lift ahead of this feature spread across portions of Souh Texas during the afternoon, atop airmass that was moist and unstable.  Sufficient deep layer shear resulted in isolated severe storms during the afternoon across South Texas.  Evetually an organized MCS developed and swept across South Texas during the evening hours.  Southwesterly mid level winds of 40-50 kts atop a strenghtening low level jet in the evening across South Texas resulted in both bowing line segments and supercells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some city streets were reported flooded in Sinton.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated supercell thunderstorms formed in the late afternoon and early evening hours across McMullen county.  The storms tracked southwest into the instability axis, through northern and western Duval county and into eastern and southern Webb county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado was observed over open brush country just west of Highway 16 near F.M. 624, moving east.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms during the evening hours produced numerous tornadoes and large hail across the southern...central and eastern Texas panhandle. Heavy rains caused flash flooding in the eastern Texas panhandle during the late night and early morning hours. One man was killed by a tornado in the northeastern Texas panhandle and another person was injured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported at Bell Street and Loop 335 (Hollywood Road).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms during the evening hours produced numerous tornadoes and large hail across the southern...central and eastern Texas panhandle. Heavy rains caused flash flooding in the eastern Texas panhandle during the late night and early morning hours. One man was killed by a tornado in the northeastern Texas panhandle and another person was injured.

Numerous severe thunderstorms producing large hail...damaging winds...and tornadoes moved north and northeast across the western and central Texas panhandle during the evening hours. The only injuries occurred in Cactus from a tornado...however no fatalities were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous tree limbs blown down by the high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system moved eastward out of north Texas during the morning of June 3rd and intensified as it moved into northeast Texas.  This system was embedded in northwesterly winds aloft which are often prolific wind producers if these systems can sustain themselves.  The storm system encountered a very unstable airmass across northeast Texas and northwest Louisiana.  The result was widespread wind damage across the southern half of northeast Texas and northwest Louisiana through the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman driving along FM 226 near the town of Etoile was killed when the top of a pine tree was snapped off and went through her windshield. Reported by Departement of Public Safety.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system moved east across northern Mexico across west Texas and southeastern New Mexico.  Ahead of this system, a moist and unstable airmass was in place.  As temperatures warmed throughout the day, the atmosphere became more unstable.  Storms initiated in the Big Bend and across the mountains of southeastern New Mexico and moved north and eastward.  |The Midland county warning area saw five recorded tornadoes on the 23rd, four in New Mexico and one in Texas.  The four in New Mexico were documented by both trained Skywarn spotters and by a National Weather Service damage survey team.  The tornado in Texas was recorded by a television news/weather crew in the area.  Baseball size hail was also associated with the Texas storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel hail covered the ground in Toyah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced hail over the south central and the northwest Texas Panhandle during the late evening hours. No damage or injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported at 39th Avenue and Polk Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening hours produced hail and high winds across the western...central and southern Texas Panhandle. Very heavy rainfall from the thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding over the south central Texas Panhandle. No damage or injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water reported to be curb to curb and in the yard of a residence at the intersection of Eastern Street and 17th Avenue. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture was in place across North Texas as an upper level low pressure system moved into the western half of the state.  Large hail reports along with damaging winds and flash flooding were common throughout this twelve-hour event.

Low level moisture was in place across North Texas as an upper level low pressure system moved into the western half of the state.  Large hail reports along with damaging winds and flash flooding were common throughout this twelve-hour event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees were uprooted and several buildings damaged.  Power lines were also downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe thunderstorms developed over much of Oklahoma on the 19th and early on the 20th. These thunderstorms consolidated into a severe line of thunderstorms with very high winds. The thunderstorm line  moved south through much of northern Texas with wind damage reported over several countries. Monetary damages were estimated.
Thunderstorms over the northwest Hill Country on the evening of June 3 and moved southeastward across the north and central sections of South Central Texas through the late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms uprooted trees and damaged roofs in the Granite Shoals area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moving into west Texas interacted with a moist and unstable airmass over north Texas.  This resulted in large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew the roof off of an apartment complex in the 1300 block of Timberlake Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed during the heat of the day along surface boundaries.  Several reports of hail and one report of thunderstorm winds were received.

An upper level low lingered in the area for several days.  An unstable atmosphere helped kick off several rounds of showers and storms.  Boundaries helped focus rainfall totals over the same areas....leading to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas around Lewisville Lake were flooded, including Witt Road in Little Elm and Rose Lane south of King's Road in Hackberry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe thunderstorms developed over much of Oklahoma on the 19th and early on the 20th. These thunderstorms consolidated into a severe line of thunderstorms with very high winds. The thunderstorm line  moved south through much of northern Texas with wind damage reported over several countries. Monetary damages were estimated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several power lines were downed. Most of Henrietta was without power as a result. Monetary damages were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low lingered in the area for several days.  An unstable atmosphere helped kick off several rounds of showers and storms.  Boundaries helped focus rainfall totals over the same areas....leading to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flooding cars at Majors Field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system moved eastward out of north Texas during the morning of June 3rd and intensified as it moved into northeast Texas.  This system was embedded in northwesterly winds aloft which are often prolific wind producers if these systems can sustain themselves.  The storm system encountered a very unstable airmass across northeast Texas and northwest Louisiana.  The result was widespread wind damage across the southern half of northeast Texas and northwest Louisiana through the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines downed across the entire county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms produced heavy rain and flash flooding across a large portion of South Central Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continued periods of showers and thunderstorms through the nighttime hours produced general 3 to 4 inch rain amounts over much of Gillespie County.  Amounts as high as 6 inches were reported near Willow City and also southwest of Fredericksburg.  Most rural roads were closed through both the late evening and in the morning hours due to high water.  A young man was swept away in his vehicle at a low water crossing on Goehmann Lane near Fredericksburg about 2100 CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low pressure system situated to the west brought large scale lift to north Texas. Southerly winds brought deep rich moisture from the Gulf of Mexico. These and other factors caused numerous thunderstorms to develop. Widespread flooding and wind damage followed, as well as a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads closed and barricaded across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low, combined with abundant moisture, produced numerous showers and thunderstorms throughout the coastal communities in and around Galveston, Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado produced minor damage to a small home and garage. There was also minor shingle damage to a residence across the street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level storm system moved from the desert southwest into west Texas.  Storms developed along a dry line Sunday evening.  The main area of upper level lift along with an attendant Pacific cold front arrived Monday.  As a result, there were several reports of severe weather and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Richland Hills police department reported a 10 to 12 foot section of a  roof blew into a street and damaged a car.  Additionally, a roof was blown off a vacant building in Haltom City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms which developed across the western Texas Panhandle during the evening hours moved east across the central Texas Panhandle by late evening and the early morning hours. These severe storms produced damaging winds across an extensive area across the southern Texas Panhandle and some large hail...however no injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across northwest sections of Amarillo...the storm survey team found a few homes and businesses which suffered window and roof damage from the downburst wind event. Damage to these structures were likely the result of winds between eighty asd ninety miles an hour. An off-duty National Weather Service employee reported that a fireworks stand was blown over three quarters of a mile south of Loop 335 on North Western Street. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms which developed across the western Texas Panhandle during the evening hours moved east across the central Texas Panhandle by late evening and the early morning hours. These severe storms produced damaging winds across an extensive area across the southern Texas Panhandle and some large hail...however no injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported at Interstate 40 and Hope Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms affected Zapata County during the evening hours.  These storms were responsible for producing wind damage and large hail in Zapata County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to the size of pennies was reported by the general public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southern stream shortwave trough approaching southwestern Texas enhanced thunderstorm development along and east of the dryline.  Deep layer shear was quite strong and allowed a tornado to develop in Mitchell county.
Thunderstorms developed in the early evening across South Central Texas, producing large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms produced general 2 to 3 inch rain amounts in the late evening in the Henley area.  Several roads, including FM165, were closed in the early morning hours by high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms associated with a persistent upper level low produced heavy rain and flash flooding in the afternoon and evening of July 28.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 2 and 3 inches of rain fell in less than an hour in the Hallettsville area, causing flash flooding that was reported to have closed most streets and roads in Hallettsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms associated with a persistent upper level low produced heavy rain and flash flooding in the morning and early afternoon of July 29.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed in southern Val Verde County, spreading eastward into southern Edwards County in the early morning hours.  Rain totals over the south part of Edwards County averaged from 3 to 4 inches with the highest totals in the southwest part of the county near the Kinney County line.  Flash flooding closed FM2523 and FM674.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a southward moving outflow boundary from thunderstorms the day before and during the overnight. During the initial stages of thunderstorm development, a land spout tornado developed in northern McCulloch County. After the thunderstorm matured, the tornado dissipated.  But it started producing large hail as the storm moved further to the south across the Northwest Hill Country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golfball size hail fell for several minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture was in place across North Texas as an upper level low pressure system moved into the western half of the state.  Large hail reports along with damaging winds and flash flooding were common throughout this twelve-hour event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low and dryline interacted with a very unstable atmosphere over North Texas.  Large hail, damaging winds, and tornadoes were reported as strong storms developed.  A total of 5,500 cars and 3,500 homes and businesses were reportedly damaged by hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down near Sylvania Park, just southwest of the intersection of Beach and Belknap Streets.  Severe damage occurred on Wheeler Street from North Beach to Haltom Road, and along Elliott Reeder Road from Haltom Road east approximately a half mile.  A fatality also occurred in this area.  Substantial roof damage was done to a grocery store, several homes, and a church.  Another church suffered steeple damage.  Several commercial buildings along Elliott Reeder Road were damaged and numerous recreational vehicles and tractor-trailers were damaged or destroyed.  Trees and power lines were also downed.  Near the intersection of Riverbend and Jack Newell Roads, a commercial building suffered partial collapse of an east-facing wall.  Intermittent damage to trees, fences, and at least one home occurred in the River Trails subdivision, west of the tornado track.  The tornado dissipated just west of Precinct Line Road.  Winds speeds were estimated at 100-110 MPH.  Haltom City and Fort Worth officials estimated about two million dollars in property losses within the two cities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong upper low pressure system combined with a strong surface low moved across the Texas Panhandle into Oklahoma and Kansas and pushed a dryline through West Central Texas.  This resulted in several severe thunderstorms across the area.

Scattered thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds and very heavy rainfall over the west Texas South Plains during the evening hours of the 28th. Most damage occurred over northern Lynn County, where large hail destroyed young cotton crops. Crops also were damaged in Hockley, Swisher and Terry Counties. Slow storm movements attributed to flash flooding in Lynn and Floyd Counties that stranded motorists and threatened structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The local broadcast media relayed a report of nickel size hail along Interstate 27 on the Hale and Swisher County line south of Kress. Delayed reports in newspaper clipping from the area suggested that some minor crop damage may have occurred, but no details were included. Therefore a small amount of agricultural damage is reported here.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms initiated along a dryline that stretched north to south across west Texas.  The atmosphere east of the dryline was unstable and storms quickly became severe.

A potent upper level system moved across Texas beginning in the late morning and continued into the afternoon. This produced a Quasilinear Convective System or Line Echo Wave Pattern (LEWP) that produced widespread damaging winds and a few isolated tornadoes. South of the line, more classic supercell structure was noted with thunderstorms across the Edwards Plateau and the Northwest Hill County, however no tornadoes were reported with these thunderstorms as the storms moved over very sparsely populated areas.

A potent upper level system moved across Texas beginning in the late morning and continued into the afternoon. This produced a Quasilinear Convective System or Line Echo Wave Pattern (LEWP) that produced widespread damaging winds and a few isolated tornadoes. South of the line, more classic supercell structure was noted with thunderstorms across the Edwards Plateau and the Northwest Hill County, however no tornadoes were reported with these thunderstorms as the storms moved over very sparsely populated areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 87 MPH was measured by a Davis weather station before the anemometer broke. The survey team observed damage to several buildings and they saw numerous downed trees. An apartment wall collapsed and several mobile homes were severely damaged. The winds were part of a squall line that was moving through the area. A funnel cloud was sighted to the south of Brownwood shortly before the winds hit, and was likely associated with the tornado to the east of Brookesmith.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive heavy rainfall resulted in high water reported on Lake Palestine which boarders Smith and Cherokee counties in northeast Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake Palestine rose to a stage of 349.1 feet which was just a few inches short of its all time record crest. With the lake at this stage...the lake had to be closed to boating traffic due to the threat of boat wake damaging local camp homes along the lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Waves of intense showers and thunderstorms continued to move north through northern Texas. The heavy rainfall and already saturated ground helped cause more flash flooding through the morning and early afternoon hours. Monetary damages were estimated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: HWY 79 was closed due to continued high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developing on the evening of May 9 produced both severe weather and flash flooding in the western counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pecos River at Pandale Crossing crested at 11.73 ft at 2130 CST on May 10, where flood stage is 5 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours produced numerous tornadoes...large hail...prolonged flooding and flash flooding...and damaging winds across the central and northeastern Texas Panhandle. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported at the intersection of State Highway 70 and Farm to Market Road 281.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in the afternoon. The  thunderstorms affected the Northern Edwards Plateau, the Heartland and the Northwest Hill Country. Another isolated supercell developed in Throckmorton County and produced another tornado. There were other tornadoes reported with the southern storms across the Edwards Plateau and the Northwest Hill Country.
Severe thunderstorms across the northeastern and southeastern Texas Panhandle produced hail and damaging winds during the evening hours. No injuries were reported.

A potent middle and upper atmospheric storm system moved eastward over the west Texas South Plains on the 17th. This system resulted in the development of numerous thunderstorms across the region. With abundant sunshine and heating that occurred early in the day beneath very cold mid-level temperatures aloft, steep vertical temperature lapse rates developed. With strong instability in place, thunderstorms developed rapidly around midday in the vicinity of a stalled frontal boundary that stretched from northwest to southeast across the region.|Thunderstorm updrafts were enhanced by the cold mid-level temperatures, and this made them efficient for stretching small circulations along the frontal boundary. This process, combined with complex storm interactions and multi-cell storm mergers, resulted in the development of at least six tornadoes across the South Plains region. All of the tornadoes were weak, but some damage did occur in Hale and Lubbock Counties, including within the city of Lubbock. The storms also produced large hail up to the size of ping pong balls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a swath of hail up to the size of nickels across northwestern Garza County. Hail accumulated five inches deep on U.S. Highway 84 by 13:30 CST. Snow-plows were used to remove the hail from the driving surface. Residents of northern Garza County continued to report large hail as the storm moved east of the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A split in the upper levels of the atmosphere steered several southern stream impulses into north Texas, producing flash flooding, strong winds, and a few tornadoes.  The most impressive of these events was a derecho which moved through North Texas on May 2.  It produced winds in excess of 85 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a bridge on CR 326 and a culvert on CR 354 to wash out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A split in the upper levels of the atmosphere steered several southern stream impulses into north Texas, producing flash flooding, strong winds, and a few tornadoes.  The most impressive of these events was a derecho which moved through North Texas on May 2.  It produced winds in excess of 85 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dozens of trees, large tree limbs, power poles, and power lines were downed across the western half of the county.  The roof of a barn was blown off onto State Highway 11, and a tree fell on a house on Cranford Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported several wind gusts up to 76 mph.  No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing Station at Wink (KINK) recorded several wind gusts of 60 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical thunderstorms reformed during the night across West Central Texas. These thunderstorms produced very heavy rains in a short period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was significant street flooding reported in Brownwood as several of the streets were reportedly closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few reports of wind damage and hail were reported as scattered thunderstorms developed ahead of an upper level low pressure system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shortwave progressing through upper level southerly flow combined with instability along an eastward mixing dryline to produce severe thunderstorms across west Texas and southeastern New Mexico.  A strong low level jet and an approaching cold front increased both instability and lift.  |Though a tornado watch was issued for much of the Permian Basin and the upper Trans-Pecos, large hail ended up being the primary threat.  No tornadoes were reported with these storms.
Showers and thunderstorms moved through Southeast Texas during the day and produced some severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Haines Road washed out with creek flooding in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds and hail across the western and central sections of South Central Texas.

A line of severe thunderstorms formed between Crystal City and Carrizo Springs and began to move eastward.  As the line crossed into Bexar County near midnight, it began to bow forward and accelerate, producing widespread wind damage.
Thunderstorms on April 30 produced widespread flash flooding along with spotty severe weather in the central and east counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds blew over trees at San Antonio College near downtown San Antonio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system located over northern portions of Mexico in combination with east to southeast surface winds pulling in abundant amounts of Gulf moisture and providing upslope flow, led to the development of showers and thunderstorms across west Texas and southeastern New Mexico. |During the overnight hours on early Sunday morning, showers and thunderstorms moved out of northern Mexico into southwest Texas.  Additional thunderstorms developed over the higher elevations of southwest Texas leading to pre-sunrise severe weather in the form of hail and flash flooding across the southwest portions of the County Warning Area.  As the morning progressed, a large area of embedded showers and thunderstorms moved northeastward across southeastern New Mexico and the Texas Permian Basin producing occasional severe weather, before exiting into the South Plains of West Texas.|With antecedent conditions remaining in place, the additional boundaries left by morning convection and daytime heating led to thunderstorm re-development by early Sunday afternoon and a significant severe weather event that affected much of the area into the pre-dawn hours on Monday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the late morning of April 29, the Jeff Davis County emergency manager reported a tornado just south of the intersection of Texas Highways 166 and 17 over open country.  While several hundred yards wide, the tornado was described by several spotters as having a relatively weak rotation.  This is confirmed by the lack of damage as the tornado crossed both Highway 17 and Highway 166.  The tornado moved along a north northwest track, just missing most of the the town of Fort Davis.  The tornado then moved into Davis Mountains State Park, across Skyline Drive near Scenic Outlook, and then down the mountain into the lower elevations on the east side of the park.  Tree damage was noted both on the mountain side and in the valley areas.  As the tornado crossed Highway 118, it began to meander along Limpia Creek before finally dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding rainfall continued to spread across the region on July 9th as the upper trough of low pressure remained parked across our region. Plentiful moisture combined with daytime heating allowed for scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms across southeast Oklahoma, southwest Arkansas, northwest Louisiana and northeast Texas resulting in flood producing rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees downed...one blocking all four lanes of US Hwy. 67.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of showers and thunderstorms developed during the morning hours of August 1st across portions of east central Texas. Light steering winds resulted in very slow movement to these storms. As a result... flash flooding was reported across Nacogdoches County, Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported across the northern part of the county. High water was reported across county roads 940...915 and 204 near Trawick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms associated with a persistent upper level low produced heavy rain and flash flooding on the afternoon and evening of July 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced general rain totals of 2 to 3 inches over Kinney, Uvalde, Zavala, Medina, and Frio Counties in the afternoon of July 3. Flash flooding in Kinney County near Brackettville closed FM693 and FM334.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A split in the upper levels of the atmosphere steered several southern stream impulses into north Texas, producing flash flooding, strong winds, and a few tornadoes.  The most impressive of these events was a derecho which moved through North Texas on May 2.  It produced winds in excess of 85 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large limbs down in Bedford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system interacted with a moist and unstable airmass over north Texas, resulting in large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding.
